lines.text: This is Tucker Traywick, PRGE field unit, broadcasting mobile from the Near East Side. It's {{CLOCK}}. I'm on foot. Signal's clean for now.
Let's do the numbers. Madison-wide kill count as of {{CLOCK}} is sitting at thirty-one. That's down nine percent from this hour last year, which was thirty-four. Dane County total is thirty-eight.
The delta between city and county has been narrowing since 2165 — used to be a fifteen-point spread, now it's seven. Urbanization of Purge activity. I've got a chart. You can't see it. Trust me.
Neighborhood breakdown. East Side — Atwood corridor through Schenk's Corners — is leading at eleven. That's consistent. Atwood has led this metric for six consecutive years.
It's population density plus low median income plus limited egress routes. It's not complicated. People act surprised every year and the math has not changed.
Near West — Monroe Street through Vilas — sitting at four. That's actually up. Monroe usually stays quiet until midnight. Could be an anomaly, could be early escalation. I'll update at eleven.

I'm passing Ha Long Bay on Atwood right now. Place is boarded up, obviously. They do a lemongrass pho that — it's genuinely one of the best things I've eaten in this city. I think about it more than I should.
I hope they make it through tonight. The restaurant, I mean. The building. The people too, obviously. All of it.
Sorry. Moving on.
Downtown — State Street through Capitol Square — is at three. Campus-adjacent zones always start slow. Students either evacuate or barricade in the dorms.
The university runs a shelter program now, which has dropped student casualty rates forty-two percent since 2162. That's one of the few numbers that's actually gotten better.

South Side — Park Street through the Beltline — six. Fish Hatchery Road specifically is at three of those six. If you're on Fish Hatchery right now — I don't know what to tell you. Get off Fish Hatchery. That's not data, that's advice.

I'm checking my rig. Comms are solid. I've got the Motorola on channel nine for Tim, the scanner on county dispatch — which goes quiet at 19:00 officially but there's always bleed — and the body cam is recording.

Battery's at seventy-one percent. I swapped to the new lithium cells this year. The old NiMH ones were dying by 2am. You can't do field work with dead batteries. That's not a metaphor. That's logistics.

Wind's picking up from the southwest, about twelve knots. Caroline would have the exact number. I've got an anemometer on my pack — it's a Kestrel 5500, handheld, measures wind, temp, humidity, barometric pressure. I don't need all that data but I like having it. Sue me.

Let me give you the year-over-year. At this point last year — three hours in, {{ELAPSED}} to be exact — we were at thirty-four city, forty-one county. Year before that, twenty-eight city, thirty-nine county.

Five-year average for this hour is thirty-one point four. So tonight is tracking exactly on the mean. Statistically unremarkable. I say that knowing that each of those thirty-one data points is a person. I know that. I always know that.
I'm crossing Winnebago Street now. There's a group — I can see flashlights, maybe four, five people — moving north on First Street. I'm going to take a different route.

That's not fear, that's probability management. A solo unit encountering a group of five is a negative-expected-value engagement regardless of armament. I'm not armed tonight. I'm never armed. The data is the job. The gun would make it a different job.

I want to talk about the weapons distribution for a second because the numbers are interesting. Last year's post-Purge audit — and these are Dane County coroner numbers, not mine — showed sixty-one percent firearm, twenty-three percent edged weapon, nine percent blunt force, seven percent other.

That seven percent "other" category is where it gets strange. It includes vehicular, incendiary, and three cases classified as "improvised." I requested clarification on "improvised." I did not receive it.

The firearm percentage has been climbing about one point per year since 2160. It was fifty-three percent in 2160. It's functionally a gun event now. The edged-weapon romantics are a shrinking demographic. Take that however you want.

Tim, if you're listening — and you're always listening — I'm going to loop south through Marquette and come back up through Jenifer Street. Signal might get spotty near the Yahara. I'll check in at the top of the hour.

For everyone at home — the numbers are the numbers. I don't editorialize. I don't tell you what to feel about them. I count, I report, I move. That's the job. Tucker Traywick, PRGE field unit. Back at eleven.

tickerItems.text: East Side (Atwood–Schenk's Corners) leading at 11 kills through {{CLOCK}}. Consistent with 6-year trend.
UW-Madison shelter program reports 94% dorm occupancy. Student casualty rate down 42% since 2162.
Fish Hatchery Rd (South Side) at 3 kills. Tucker advises: get off Fish Hatchery.
Text from Marquette listener: 'Tucker just walked past my house. They looked calm. I am not calm.'
Weapons audit YoY: firearm use at 61%, up from 53% in 2160. Edged weapons declining at 23%.
Text from Atwood listener: 'Ha Long Bay pho is incredible. Tucker has taste.'
